title: What Your Miles Are Worth: A Guide to Asian and Gulf Loyalty Programmes
dek: KrisFlyer, Asia Miles, Skywards, Privilege Club and Air India's Maharaja Club all promise rewards, but a mile is not a fixed currency. Here is how to think about frequent-flyer value across the region.
body:
Frequent-flyer programmes are among the most valuable assets in modern aviation, yet the points they hand out behave less like cash and more like a fluctuating currency. A mile earned with Singapore Airlines, Cathay Pacific, Emirates, Qatar Airways or Air India can be worth wildly different amounts depending on how it is redeemed. For travellers across Asia and the Gulf weighing which programme to commit to, the key is to focus on value rather than the raw size of a balance.

Why a mile has no single value

The worth of a point depends almost entirely on the redemption. The same balance might cover a modest discount on an economy ticket or, used carefully, a premium-cabin seat that would cost many times more in cash. Programmes also differ in structure: Singapore's KrisFlyer, Cathay's membership built around Asia Miles, Emirates Skywards, Qatar Airways' Privilege Club, which uses Avios, and Air India's Maharaja Club each set their own earning rates, award charts and partner options.

Several factors swing the real return up or down, and they are worth checking before chasing any single airline:

  • Premium-cabin and partner-airline redemptions usually deliver the highest value per point.
  • Taxes, carrier-imposed surcharges and dynamic pricing can erode the benefit of an award seat.
  • Elite status, not just points, drives perks such as lounge access, upgrades and baggage allowances.

Choosing a programme that fits

The best programme is the one that matches a traveller's actual flying. Someone based around a Gulf hub may find Skywards or Privilege Club most useful, while a traveller routing through Southeast or East Asia might lean toward KrisFlyer or an Asia Miles balance. Alliance membership matters too, since Star Alliance, Oneworld and SkyTeam partners widen where points can be earned and burned.

The cautious approach is to avoid hoarding points indefinitely, watch for expiry rules and devaluations, and treat miles as something to use thoughtfully rather than collect for their own sake. Concentrating activity in one or two well-chosen programmes generally beats spreading thin across many.
